Human evaluation of practical research value
Conference acceptance level assessment
Ablation study component effectiveness analysis
key_results: 10.67% improvement in research idea quality compared to other methods; generated ideas surpass typical top-tier conference acceptance standards; human evaluation confirmed practical value for scientific research; ablation studies validated effectiveness of each workflow component (explore-expand-evolve workflow, Idea Stack, critic engine).
relevance_to_hypothesis: Directly supports hypothesis: Deep Ideation grounds novelty through multiple mechanisms (literature_comparison via scientific concept network, small_experiments via critic engine trained on reviewer feedback, induction via pattern learning from papers), demonstrating that grounded ideation produces higher quality ideas (10.67% improvement) compared to unguided/less grounded baselines.
limitations: Abstract and available documentation do not specify: (1) exact dataset sizes for scientific literature corpus or reviewer feedback dataset; (2) specific baseline paper names/methods used in comparison; (3) computational requirements or scalability limitations; (4) scope of domains/fields tested; (5) detailed metric definitions beyond quality percentage; (6) human evaluation sample size or inter-rater agreement; (7) whether ideas were actually implemented or only assessed by reviewing.

one_liner: Survey of 5 families of LLM-based scientific idea generation methods (knowledge augmentation, prompt steering, inference-time scaling, multi-agent collaboration, parameter adaptation) mapped to Boden/Rhodes creativity frameworks to analyze novelty-validity tradeoffs.
problem: Scientific idea generation requires joint satisfaction of novelty (departure from existing knowledge) and valueness (correctness, plausibility, feasibility). Current LLMs struggle to balance creative novelty with scientific soundness, and evaluation remains subjective/non-standardized. Existing methods predominantly achieve combinatorial or exploratory creativity, with transformational creativity elusive.
method: Systematic survey synthesizing 5 method families: (1) External knowledge augmentation via semantic/relational retrieval (RAG, knowledge graphs); (2) Prompt-based distributional steering (persona priming, constraint-based queries, structured prompts, multilingual approaches); (3) Inference-time scaling through local sequential refinement, population-based search (beam), and tree-structured exploration; (4) Multi-agent collaboration simulating team dynamics (debate, critique, role specialization); (5) Parameter-level adaptation (fine-tuning, RLHF, preference optimization). Analysis uses Boden taxonomy (combinatorial/exploratory/transformational creativity) and Rhodes 4Ps framework (Person/Process/Press/Product).
grounding_notes: The survey demonstrates multiple grounding mechanisms: (1) Literature_comparison: RAG/semantic retrieval and relational knowledge graphs enable novelty assessment by exposing structure invisible to nearest-neighbor search (GoAI achieves 3.83 novelty vs Chain-of-Ideas 3.21 vs vanilla 2.44). (2) Deduction: Chain-of-Ideas and Bit-Flip-Spark use theory->prediction reasoning (baseline assumption, flip, spark insight). (3) Induction: Program-level ideation generates structured protocols tested via simulators with results looped back as reward signals. (4) Small_experiments: Robin's lab-in-the-loop tests hypotheses via in silico simulators; ChemCrow validates reaction pathways using cheminformatics tools; SCaSML integrates physical laws as constraints. (5) Outcome_imagination: Scientific priors constrain generation to plausible bounds; feedback sources (self-evaluation, peer LLMs, simulators, external tools, human-in-the-loop) guide exploration toward feasible outcomes. (6) Proposal_writing: Structured creative prompts scaffold systematic ideation with explicit reasoning steps. Key insight: grounding in structured knowledge enables novel recombinations while preventing arbitrary ideas—the press (environmental context) dimension contributes novelty precisely because external knowledge introduces novelty while preserving correctness.
datasets: SciMuse (100 research group leaders for evaluation)
GoAI (Graph of AI Ideas - large-scale AI research idea generation study)
metrics: Elo-based novelty scoring
Average composite score (novelty+valueness)
Originality via TTCT-inspired framework
Fluency, flexibility, elaboration (valueness dimensions)
Diversity of generated ideas
Feasibility (resource requirements)
Utility/impact assessment
Human expert evaluation
LLM-as-a-Judge (prompt-based, fine-tuned, RL-optimized variants)
key_results: GoAI (structured graph-based ideation): novelty 3.83, average 3.39 vs Chain-of-Ideas: novelty 3.21, average 3.27 vs vanilla LLM prompting: novelty 2.44, average 2.70. Persona prompting shows novelty 4.23, valueness 4.62; Chain-of-Thought shows valueness 4.75, novelty 3.76. Knowledge augmentation supports combinatorial creativity through novel concept recombinations. Inference-time scaling emphasizes process dimension. Multi-agent collaboration fosters emergent cross-disciplinary thinking. Current methods remain at combinatorial/exploratory levels; transformational creativity elusive. Key tradeoffs: higher retrieval quality reduces diversity (overfitting to passages); RLHF reduces entropy pushing toward safer outputs; confidence miscalibration in self-evaluation; risk of reward hacking in peer evaluators.
relevance_to_hypothesis: HIGHLY RELEVANT. This survey directly validates the hypothesis that LLMs generate higher-quality scientific ideas when novelty is grounded through structured mechanisms vs unguided generation. Evidence: (1) GoAI's structured graph-based approach (literature_comparison + relational knowledge grounding) dramatically outperforms vanilla LLM (3.39 vs 2.70 average). (2) Multiple grounding mechanisms systematically discussed—knowledge augmentation (literature_comparison), Bit-Flip-Spark (deduction), simulator feedback (small_experiments), scientific priors (outcome_imagination), structured prompts (proposal_writing). (3) Survey explicitly frames grounding as preventing 'arbitrary or incoherent ideas' while enabling novel recombinations. (4) Five method families each represent different grounding approaches, all contrasted against unguided baselines. (5) Rhodes 4Ps framework emphasizes press (environmental/contextual grounding) as creativity source. The survey's core thesis—that joint satisfaction of novelty AND valueness requires grounding—directly supports hypothesis that grounding mechanisms improve scientific idea quality beyond pure novelty.
limitations: Survey is organizational/taxonomic rather than empirical—synthesizes existing literature without conducting new experiments. Lacks comprehensive standardized benchmarks for scientific ideation evaluation; most evaluation remains subjective/domain-specific or relies on LLM-as-Judge (potentially biased). Limited explicit dataset sizes/specifications beyond SciMuse (100 evaluators) and general references to knowledge sources. No GitHub links or reproducible code provided. Evaluation metrics not consistently applied across different method families. Authors acknowledge: (1) current architectural constraints (autoregressive/attention-based) may fundamentally limit transformational creativity; (2) trade-offs in grounding approaches (e.g., RAG reduces diversity despite improving retrieval quality); (3) RLHF training paradoxically reduces creative output entropy; (4) self-confidence miscalibration problem; (5) reward hacking risks in peer/simulator evaluators. Future work needed on agent-level search, domain-rich simulators, and shifting evaluation paradigms.

one_liner: First large-scale head-to-head comparison of LLM vs 100+ expert NLP researchers shows LLM-generated research ideas are rated significantly more novel (p<0.05) but slightly weaker on feasibility, while revealing critical limitations in diversity and self-evaluation.
problem: Whether current LLMs can generate novel, expert-level research ideas comparable to human researchers. This is the foundational step of scientific discovery and essential for autonomous research agents to perform ideation tasks.
method: Head-to-head experimental design with blind peer review comparing human experts vs LLM ideation agent. 49 expert NLP researchers recruited to write prompting research ideas across 7 topics (Bias, Coding, Safety, Multilingual, Factuality, Math, Uncertainty). 79 expert reviewers (104 total unique participants) provided blind reviews. Three conditions: human-written ideas (N=49), AI-generated ideas (N=49), AI+human-reranked ideas (N=49). LLM agent: retrieval-augmented (k=10 papers prepended), generates 4000 ideas/topic, deduplicates at 0.8 cosine similarity, ranks via Claude-3.5-Sonnet pairwise comparisons (N=5 Swiss tournament rounds trained on 1200 ICLR 2024 submissions). Statistical testing: Welch's t-tests with Bonferroni correction across three independent designs (reviews/ideas/reviewers as datapoints).
grounding_notes: Primary grounding: literature comparison via retrieval augmentation (top-10 papers by title+abstract prepended to generation prompt). Secondary grounding: induction (LLM explicitly asked to avoid repetitions via previous idea titles in prompt), deduction (Swiss tournament pairwise ranking trained on ICLR 2024 acceptance data), proposal_writing (structured template enforced for all ideas). Critical limitation revealed: retrieval-augmented grounding insufficient—only 200 unique ideas from 4000 attempts per topic (5% diversity). Pairwise ranking achieves only 53.3% consistency vs human 56.1%, indicating LLM cannot reliably evaluate grounded ideas. Paper identifies fundamental gap: grounding through literature retrieval and proposal structure does not prevent quality degradation during ranking/filtering phases.
datasets: Human idea writing: 49 ideas from expert NLP researchers on 7 topics
ICLR 2024 LLM-related submissions (N=1200) used to train pairwise ranker
4000 generated seed ideas per topic (7 topics; ~200 unique per topic after deduplication at 0.8 threshold)
baselines: Human expert researchers: 49 PhD/postdoc writers from 26 institutions (avg 12 papers, 477 citations, h-index 5)
Human expert reviewers: 79 reviewers from 32 institutions, mostly PhD/postdoc (avg 15 papers, 635 citations, h-index 7; 72/79 previously reviewed for major venues)
LLM rankers tested: Claude-3.5-Sonnet (71.4% pairwise accuracy on ICLR data), GPT-4o (61.1%), Claude-3-Opus (63.5%)
LLM-as-judge baselines: Direct score prediction, pairwise comparison, AI Scientist reviewer agent
metrics: Novelty Score (1-10 scale): AI Ideas μ=5.64±σ=1.76 vs Human μ=4.84±σ=1.79 (p<0.01); AI+Rerank μ=5.81±σ=1.66 (p<0.001)
Excitement Score (1-10): AI μ=5.19±1.73 vs Human μ=4.55±1.89 (p<0.05); AI+Rerank μ=5.46±1.82 (p<0.01)
Feasibility Score (1-10): AI μ=6.34±1.88 vs Human μ=6.61±1.99 (p=1.00, not significant)
Expected Effectiveness Score (1-10): All comparable (AI μ=5.47; Human μ=5.13)
Inter-reviewer consistency (balanced accuracy): Ours 56.1%; NeurIPS'21 66.0%; ICLR'24 71.9%; Human-AI agreement: GPT-4o Direct 50.0%, Claude-3.5 Pairwise 53.3%
Idea duplication rate: 95% duplication at 4000 ideas per topic (only 5% unique retained)
key_results: AI-generated ideas rated significantly more novel than human expert ideas across all three statistical tests (Novelty difference: AI +0.80 points p<0.01; AI+Rerank +0.97 p<0.001). Excitement also significantly higher for AI (p<0.05 and p<0.01). Feasibility slightly lower for AI but not significant (p=1.00). Collected 298 unique expert reviews (avg 3.8 reviews/reviewer, 32 min/review, 232 words). Human experts report their submitted ideas ~top 43% of all past ideas (median-level due to 10-day constraint; 37/49 generated on-the-spot). Novelty and excitement drive overall score (r=0.725 and r=0.854) but feasibility barely correlates (r=0.097). Qualitative failures: vague implementation details, dataset misuse, insufficient literature grounding. LLM ranker 71.4% accurate on ICLR but only 53.3% consistent on collected ideas. Manual human reranking outperforms LLM on 32/49 ideas, showing significant LLM-human selection discrepancy. Reviewers achieved only 56.1% inter-agreement (lower than conference reviewing 66-72%).
code_links: https://github.com/NoviScl/AI-Researcher (agent implementation and human review scores)
https://tinyurl.com/execution-study (follow-up study recruiting researchers to execute ideas)
relevance_to_hypothesis: Highly relevant. Paper directly tests whether LLM-generated ideas are superior when grounded through literature comparison and proposal structure vs ungrounded human ideation. Results support hypothesis that grounding via retrieval+prompting yields higher perceived novelty (p<0.05). However, reveals critical nuance: grounding mechanisms improve novelty perception but do NOT prevent two major failure modes—lack of diversity (95% duplication) and unreliable self-evaluation (53.3% LLM judge vs 56.1% human consistency). Suggests grounding novelty generation is orthogonal to ensuring genuine originality and sound evaluation. AI+Human Rerank outperforms pure AI ranker, indicating grounding operates at generation-time but fails during ranking/filtering. This is key insight: grounding improves idea quality perception at generation but cannot substitute for expert evaluation of grounded ideas.
limitations: Study scope limited to prompting-based NLP research only (not generalizable to other domains). Novelty/feasibility evaluated on written ideas only, not executed research—subjective judgment, not real outcomes. Inter-reviewer consistency only 56.1% vs conference baseline 66-72%, raising questions about evaluation validity. Human participants primarily US-based due to logistics (US-biased sample). Human experts submitted median-level ideas (~top 43%) within 10-day constraint, not best ideas (downward bias on human baseline). LLM agent uses only Claude-3.5-Sonnet for ranking; generalization unclear (GPT-4o, Opus lower accuracy on ICLR data). Deduplication threshold 0.8 set via manual inspection; sensitivity unexplored. Sample size modest (N=49/condition); insufficient power for feasibility/effectiveness sub-analyses. No control for prompt engineering, temperature, decoding strategy. Confounding: AI ideas longer (1186 words) vs human (902 words)—may inflate novelty perception. EMNLP 2024 overlap analysis pre-registered but results pending.
